paragraphs.text: Spotify
SPOTIFY RESPONSE TO THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION'S CONSULTATION FOR THE DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

Spotify was founded in Sweden by Daniel Ek in 2006, a time when the music industry was ravaged by piracy. We thought that if people could access the music they want, when they want, many would be willing to pay for it and we could help give artists the opportunity to live off their art.

section_path: INTRODUCTION

evidence_quotes.quote: personalised recommendations that help them to discover new content they love.

paragraphs.text: Fast-forward nearly 20 years and not only is streaming driving record revenues into the music industry, more artists are sharing in that success than ever before. By lowering barriers to entry, streaming empowers artists to distribute their music widely and to have a chance at commercial success. The success of music streaming with consumers is driven by freedom of choice, enabled by the on-demand nature of our service and personalised recommendations that help them to discover new content they love.

section_path: INTRODUCTION

paragraphs.text: At Spotify, we share the European Commission's goal of ensuring high standards of consumer protection and transparency. Users of Spotify should be able to enjoy music, podcast and audiobook content safely, transparently, and with confidence that their choices are respected.

section_path: INTRODUCTION

paragraphs.text: They should always feel in control of their experience — from the moment of sign-up and throughout their experience thereafter.

section_path: INTRODUCTION

evidence_quotes.quote: clear, straightforward cancellation process

paragraphs.text: In recent years, we have made significant investments to ensure a positive consumer experience on our platform — from intuitive UX design, clear pre-contractual information and a clear, straightforward cancellation process, to providing publicly available resources on data privacy and platform safety. We take particular care to protect younger listeners on Spotify, including through dedicated accounts with parental controls and built-in safeguards.

section_path: INTRODUCTION

paragraphs.text: We are glad to participate in the consultation for the assessment of a Digital Fairness Act, but we regret that the questionnaire is drafted in a binary format that does not allow us to comment on specific policy options in cases where we select "no action is needed." For this reason, we attach this paper to provide additional views and context.

section_path: INTRODUCTION

paragraphs.text: Spotify strives to align with legal standards for consumer protection in Europe and globally. As such, we are concerned by the European Commission's intention to introduce new, horizontal legislation — the proposed Digital Fairness Act (DFA). Many of the policy options proposed in the Public Consultation appear overly broad and unjustified, and would go against the EU's commitment to streamline regulation and promote competitiveness. We urge European policymakers to refocus on clarifying existing rules in ways that benefit both consumers and businesses, and on improving enforcement where violations arise.

section_path: INTRODUCTION

paragraphs.text: Spotify is present in every EU Member State, serving millions of consumers on a daily basis.

section_path: A CRITICAL ASSESSMENT OF TODAY'S REGULATORY LANDSCAPE

paragraphs.text: Our experience with Europe's consumer protection framework is that it is increasingly dense and complex.

section_path: A CRITICAL ASSESSMENT OF TODAY'S REGULATORY LANDSCAPE

evidence_quotes.quote: the Unfair Contract Terms Directive (UCTD)

paragraphs.text: Long-standing laws such as the Consumer Rights Directive (CRD), the Price Indications Directive (PID), the Unfair Contract Terms Directive (UCTD) and the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (UCPD) – all recently amended by the Enforcement and Modernisation Directive – now sit alongside substantial sectoral legislation, notably the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR),

section_path: A CRITICAL ASSESSMENT OF TODAY'S REGULATORY LANDSCAPE

paragraphs.text: e-Privacy Directive, Digital Services Act (DSA), Accessibility Act, AI Act and more.

section_path: A CRITICAL ASSESSMENT OF TODAY'S REGULATORY LANDSCAPE

paragraphs.text: The volume of regulations, combined with divergent and sometimes rigid rules at national level, creates a highly layered compliance landscape for companies to navigate while continuing to deliver innovation and consumer value.

section_path: A CRITICAL ASSESSMENT OF TODAY'S REGULATORY LANDSCAPE

paragraphs.text: The European Commission's broader goal to simplify and streamline legislation is both welcome and urgent.

section_path: A CRITICAL ASSESSMENT OF TODAY'S REGULATORY LANDSCAPE

paragraphs.text: We see consumer protection as a priority area for the Commission's broader simplification agenda.

section_path: A CRITICAL ASSESSMENT OF TODAY'S REGULATORY LANDSCAPE

evidence_quotes.quote: did not account for the numerous digital legislations adopted in recent years which address consumer protection from different angles

paragraphs.text: While Spotify fully supports strong consumer protection standards, we question the premise that there are "gaps" in current rules. The Fitness Check underpinning the DFA proposal did not account for the numerous digital legislations adopted in recent years which address consumer protection from different angles and thus seems incomplete.

section_path: CONCERNS WITH ADDITIONAL REGULATION UNDER THE DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT (DFA)

paragraphs.text: Furthermore, some of the policy options outlined in the Public Consultation would be simply new, more prescriptive rules, rather than clarifications or targeted gap-filling measures. Much of the consumer harm identified by the Commission in its Fitness Check could be addressed through updated guidance and more consistent enforcement,

section_path: CONCERNS WITH ADDITIONAL REGULATION UNDER THE DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT (DFA)

paragraphs.text: rather than by enacting new laws – which may exacerbate legal confusion, would be counterproductive for consumers, and would likely also suffer from inconsistent enforcement.

section_path: CONCERNS WITH ADDITIONAL REGULATION UNDER THE DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT (DFA)

paragraphs.text: We also have serious reservations about certain policy options outlined in the Public Consultation. Some of the proposed measures are overly vague and would be disproportionate if applied uniformly to all digital services. If taken forward, they risk producing sterile, less engaging online experiences for consumers and significantly reducing the competitiveness of European tech businesses.

section_path: CONCERNS WITH ADDITIONAL REGULATION UNDER THE DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT (DFA)

evidence_quotes.quote: subscriptions contracts and consumer autonomy

paragraphs.text: We have grouped our comments in four categories: (1) sections and questions in the Consultation that concern product innovation; (2) sections and questions that concern subscriptions contracts and consumer autonomy, (3) questions concerning child safety online; and (4) horizontal consumer law issues. We refer to the relevant questions under each point.

section_path: Comments on specific policy options outlined in the Public Consultation

evidence_quotes.quote: personalised playlists and recommendations

paragraphs.text: In digital markets, product innovation is a strong driver of consumer value. Particularly for services like Spotify, which do not offer exclusive catalogues and are similarly priced to their competitors, it is constant product innovation that attracts and retains consumer interest. Some of Spotify's most well-known and loved features — from personalised playlists and recommendations to real-time lyrics — are also a proven engine of discovery and cultural diversity.

section_path: Comments on specific policy options outlined in the Public Consultation
1. Protecting Product Innovation Within a Robust Consumer Law Framework

paragraphs.text: EU legislation should therefore strike a careful balance: safeguard users' rights without constraining product innovation.

section_path: Comments on specific policy options outlined in the Public Consultation
1. Protecting Product Innovation Within a Robust Consumer Law Framework

paragraphs.text: Regarding the following policy options outlined in the Public Consultation:

section_path: Comments on specific policy options outlined in the Public Consultation
1. Protecting Product Innovation Within a Robust Consumer Law Framework

evidence_quotes.quote: Addictive design features should be switched off by default

paragraphs.text: (a) "Addictive design features should be switched off by default" — with autoplay and recommender systems that are steered to increase the consumer's engagement cited as examples of addictive design (Section 2: Addictive Design, Q.2)

section_path: Comments on specific policy options outlined in the Public Consultation
1. Protecting Product Innovation Within a Robust Consumer Law Framework

paragraphs.text: We are committed to ensuring that time spent on Spotify is time well spent. Enjoying a favourite song, audiobook or podcast should enrich listeners' day — not be a cause for concern to policymakers.

section_path: Comments on specific policy options outlined in the Public Consultation
1. Protecting Product Innovation Within a Robust Consumer Law Framework

evidence_quotes.quote: legitimate design features that consumers value with genuinely addictive functionality.

paragraphs.text: We are concerned that the Public Consultation appears to conflate legitimate design features that consumers value with genuinely addictive functionality.

section_path: Comments on specific policy options outlined in the Public Consultation
1. Protecting Product Innovation Within a Robust Consumer Law Framework

evidence_quotes.quote: Spotify features such as personalised playlists — powered by recommender systems — and continuous playback are not only appreciated by listeners; they are expected.
Moreover, when we ask users what they like most about Spotify, 81% cite our personalisation.

paragraphs.text: Spotify features such as personalised playlists — powered by recommender systems — and continuous playback are not only appreciated by listeners; they are expected. Imagine having to select "play" each time you wanted to hear a different song on Spotify. Moreover, when we ask users what they like most about Spotify, 81% cite our personalisation. Disabling such features by default would fundamentally alter the nature of the Spotify experience, making it less engaging, harming European artists from being

section_path: Comments on specific policy options outlined in the Public Consultation
1. Protecting Product Innovation Within a Robust Consumer Law Framework

paragraphs.text: discovered by new fans, and reducing consumers' willingness to pay for music subscriptions over free alternatives.

section_path: Comments on specific policy options outlined in the Public Consultation
1. Protecting Product Innovation Within a Robust Consumer Law Framework

evidence_quotes.quote: It is vitally important that the Commission clearly distinguish between genuinely harmful practices and legitimate product features that enhance engagement and user experience.

paragraphs.text: It is vitally important that the Commission clearly distinguish between genuinely harmful practices and legitimate product features that enhance engagement and user experience. Such clarification should be informed by robust evidence and behavioural research. Most crucially, EU policy must avoid blanket restrictions on widely used product features that consumers expect and enjoy.

section_path: Comments on specific policy options outlined in the Public Consultation
1. Protecting Product Innovation Within a Robust Consumer Law Framework

evidence_quotes.quote: addictive design

paragraphs.text: We would also note that concerns around "addictive design" are already addressed in existing EU legislation adjacent to consumer law, notably in the DSA and the AI Act. We encourage the Commission to undertake a more thorough assessment of how existing rules operate in practice to identify any genuine regulatory gaps before considering new policy measures of the type outlined above.

section_path: Comments on specific policy options outlined in the Public Consultation
1. Protecting Product Innovation Within a Robust Consumer Law Framework

evidence_quotes.quote: Presenting choices in a leading manner, for example, to prioritise an option for a given choice by using a brighter colour or larger font.

paragraphs.text: (b) “Presenting choices in a leading manner, for example, to prioritise an option for a given choice by using a brighter colour or larger font” (Section 1: Dark Patterns, Q.2)

section_path: Comments on specific policy options outlined in the Public Consultation
1. Protecting Product Innovation Within a Robust Consumer Law Framework

evidence_quotes.quote: guiding users through online interfaces is inherently harmful

paragraphs.text: As currently framed, this measure is overly broad and unintentionally captures lawful practices that serve consumers' interests. It also appears to assume that guiding users through online interfaces is inherently harmful, which is not the case.

section_path: Comments on specific policy options outlined in the Public Consultation
1. Protecting Product Innovation Within a Robust Consumer Law Framework

evidence_quotes.quote: Aiding consumer navigation, including by differentiating between different actions they may take, is a common and reasonable practice that can be helpful to consumers if done responsibly.

paragraphs.text: Aiding consumer navigation, including by differentiating between different actions they may take, is a common and reasonable practice that can be helpful to consumers if done responsibly.

section_path: Comments on specific policy options outlined in the Public Consultation
1. Protecting Product Innovation Within a Robust Consumer Law Framework

evidence_quotes.quote: facilitate consumer navigation through our cancellation flow.

paragraphs.text: For example, Spotify has taken steps to facilitate consumer navigation through our cancellation flow. Other service providers may highlight an option in order to have a consumer confirm critical information (e.g. a delivery address) before taking an action, to avoid unintended errors. As currently framed, this policy measure would unintentionally capture lawful and legitimate practices that are in the interests of the consumer and vital to smooth user experiences.

section_path: Comments on specific policy options outlined in the Public Consultation
1. Protecting Product Innovation Within a Robust Consumer Law Framework

evidence_quotes.quote: manipulation of consumer knowledge and choice
"signposting" using colours and fonts

paragraphs.text: Laws to prevent manipulation of consumer knowledge and choice already exist. A blank prohibition on preventing helpful "signposting" using colours and fonts would likely harm consumers – particularly those who rely on non-traditional means of comprehension such as colours and intuitive design rather than plain text.

section_path: Comments on specific policy options outlined in the Public Consultation
1. Protecting Product Innovation Within a Robust Consumer Law Framework

evidence_quotes.quote: Spotify users can cancel their Premium plan any time on their account page.

paragraphs.text: Consumers should always understand what they are signing up for and be able to cancel easily at any time. Spotify users can cancel their Premium plan any time on their account page.

section_path: 2 Subscription Contract Management and Consumer Autonomy

paragraphs.text: In that regard, we urge caution with respect to the following options:

section_path: 2 Subscription Contract Management and Consumer Autonomy

evidence_quotes.quote: Consumers should have an easy functionality (such as a cancellation link or a button) on the trader's interface to exercise their right to cancel the online contract

paragraphs.text: a) "Consumers should have an easy functionality (such as a cancellation link or a button) on the trader's interface to exercise their right to cancel the online contract" (Section 7: Issues with Digital Contracts, Q.2)

section_path: 2 Subscription Contract Management and Consumer Autonomy

evidence_quotes.quote: Mandating a "cancellation button" or equivalent product feature is excessively prescriptive and would limit businesses' freedom to design user experiences that make sense.
The principle of easy cancellation is well-established in EU law.

paragraphs.text: The principle of easy cancellation is well-established in EU law. Mandating a "cancellation button" or equivalent product feature is excessively prescriptive and would limit businesses' freedom to design user experiences that make sense. This type of measure is also likely to be quickly outpaced by evolving consumer expectations and user interfaces. We have seen the impact of detailed cancellation requirements in certain Member States. Such laws have generated significant uncertainty and litigation as businesses, consumers, consumer rights organizations and the courts discuss what is – and is not – lawful under the law.

section_path: 2 Subscription Contract Management and Consumer Autonomy

evidence_quotes.quote: obstructive cancellation

paragraphs.text: Rather than prescriptive product requirements, the Commission could update its guidance on what obstructive cancellation looks like, to inform enforcement when violations of the rules arise. Member States should also be encouraged to use their existing powers to encourage regulatory authorities to conduct market sweeps to ensure vigilance and consistent enforcement against bad practices.

section_path: 2 Subscription Contract Management and Consumer Autonomy

evidence_quotes.quote: always be reminded before their subscription is automatically renewed or a free trial is converted into a paid subscription

paragraphs.text: b) "Consumers should benefit from more transparency, e.g. always be reminded before their subscription is automatically renewed or a free trial is converted into a paid subscription" (Section 7: Issues with Digital Contracts, Q.2)

section_path: 2 Subscription Contract Management and Consumer Autonomy

evidence_quotes.quote: Auto-renewal reminders for digital subscriptions with frequent billing cycles would be both unnecessary and counterproductive.

paragraphs.text: Auto-renewal reminders for digital subscriptions with frequent billing cycles would be both unnecessary and counterproductive. In the case of Spotify, our users pay on a monthly basis and tend to be highly engaged.

section_path: 2 Subscription Contract Management and Consumer Autonomy

evidence_quotes.quote: recurring subscription payments

paragraphs.text: Repeated reminders about recurring subscription payments have been debated and disregarded in previous European policy discussions for three main reasons:

section_path: 2 Subscription Contract Management and Consumer Autonomy

evidence_quotes.quote: exacerbate the information overload consumers are already experiencing
leading them to miss or ignore genuinely important communications, for example regarding promotions or account security.

paragraphs.text: They would exacerbate the information overload consumers are already experiencing, leading them to miss or ignore genuinely important communications, for example regarding promotions or account security.

section_path: 2 Subscription Contract Management and Consumer Autonomy

paragraphs.text: Lookalike emails are prime targets for phishing, increasing consumers' vulnerability to scams.

section_path: 2 Subscription Contract Management and Consumer Autonomy

paragraphs.text: They are unnecessary provided users can cancel their subscription easily at any time.

section_path: 2 Subscription Contract Management and Consumer Autonomy

evidence_quotes.quote: before it converts into a paid subscription.

paragraphs.text: As for Free Trial customers, Spotify already notifies users seven days before their trial ends, reminding them that they may elect to cancel their trial (at no financial cost) before it converts into a paid subscription.

section_path: 2 Subscription Contract Management and Consumer Autonomy

evidence_quotes.quote: terminate an automatically extended digital subscription any time with a short notice (e.g. monthly)

paragraphs.text: c) “Consumers should have more control over their contracts, e.g. by having the possibility to terminate an automatically extended digital subscription any time with a short notice (e.g. monthly) or by having to approve explicitly the renewal of a subscription or the conversion of a free trial into a paid subscription” (Section 7: Issues with Digital Contracts, Q.2)

section_path: 2 Subscription Contract Management and Consumer Autonomy

evidence_quotes.quote: consumers should be able to cancel their Spotify subscription at any time
not be charged beyond the current month.

paragraphs.text: We agree that consumers should be able to cancel their Spotify subscription at any time and not be charged beyond the current month. We also share the Commission's view that users deserve clear, upfront information about the nature and duration of free trials.

section_path: 2 Subscription Contract Management and Consumer Autonomy

evidence_quotes.quote: before it converts into a paid subscription.

paragraphs.text: As stated above, Spotify already notifies trial users seven days before their trial ends, reminding them that they may elect to cancel their trial (at no financial cost) before it converts into a paid subscription. However, requiring explicit re-approval before a trial converts could have unintended negative consequences. Free and discounted trials benefit consumers by offering full access risk-free, helping them make informed choices before choosing whether to continue as a paying subscriber. Businesses invest significantly to provide these offers, absorbing upfront costs to build trust and demonstrate value.

section_path: 2 Subscription Contract Management and Consumer Autonomy

evidence_quotes.quote: Adding another consent step, beyond existing transparency measures, would create unnecessary friction

paragraphs.text: Adding another consent step, beyond existing transparency measures, would create unnecessary friction and risk service interruptions where consumers miss a consent request and lose access unintentionally.

section_path: 2 Subscription Contract Management and Consumer Autonomy

paragraphs.text: We urge the Commission to prioritise meaningful upfront transparency over new, prescriptive re-consent requirements.

section_path: 2 Subscription Contract Management and Consumer Autonomy

evidence_quotes.quote: right of withdrawal from digital media subscription services

paragraphs.text: d) “Re-balancing the right of withdrawal from digital media subscription services (such as audio and video streaming) to make it more sustainable for the suppliers, whilst upholding the consumers’ right to change their mind” (Section 8: Simplification Measures, Q.2)

section_path: 2 Subscription Contract Management and Consumer Autonomy

evidence_quotes.quote: waive their right of withdrawal at the point of purchase.

paragraphs.text: Spotify provides users with instant access to a global catalogue of copyright-protected music, podcasts, and audiobooks upon purchase. To make this possible, users consent to receive full access to the copyright-protected content immediately and — therefore — waive their right of withdrawal at the point of purchase. This safeguard affords right-sholders sufficient comfort to make available all of their content to consumers instantly.

section_path: 2 Subscription Contract Management and Consumer Autonomy

evidence_quotes.quote: An unconditional right of withdrawal and refund for digital media would also open the door to abuse.

paragraphs.text: We urge the Commission to preserve this system. Without it, the integrity and sustainability of digital content providers like Spotify would be undermined, requiring us to issue refunds for a service that has been delivered, while paying licensing fees to rightsholders for content streamed in that period. It would place licensed audio platforms that pay creators and rightsholders at a particular disadvantage, distorting competition in favour of unlicensed (pirate) or free alternatives that generate little to no revenue for the creative ecosystem. An unconditional right of withdrawal and refund for digital media would also open the door to abuse. Ultimately, an unintended consequence of changing the current framework could be the emergence of new barriers to the immediate access to cultural content for consumers.

section_path: 2 Subscription Contract Management and Consumer Autonomy

paragraphs.text: Spotify is a digital music, podcast, and audiobook service that provides access to millions of songs and other content from creators all over the world. We are committed to bringing the best listening experience to all our users, and that includes parents and families.

section_path: 2 Subscription Contract Management and Consumer Autonomy
3. Protecting Without Excluding: A Holistic Approach to Minors’ Access to Culture

paragraphs.text: We fully agree with the Commission that children deserve a tailored approach to ensure their safety and well-being online. We also believe that every child is different, and every family is unique. This is why we take a multi-layered approach to child safety, blending product design, policy development, enforcement protocols, and parental controls.

section_path: 2 Subscription Contract Management and Consumer Autonomy
3. Protecting Without Excluding: A Holistic Approach to Minors’ Access to Culture

paragraphs.text: As the Commission explores additional child protection measures, we urge consideration of some key principles:

section_path: 2 Subscription Contract Management and Consumer Autonomy
3. Protecting Without Excluding: A Holistic Approach to Minors’ Access to Culture

paragraphs.text: Access to creative and cultural content — particularly music — is essential to personal and social development at every stage of life.

section_path: 2 Subscription Contract Management and Consumer Autonomy
3. Protecting Without Excluding: A Holistic Approach to Minors’ Access to Culture

evidence_quotes.quote: ways that are tailored for them.

paragraphs.text: At Spotify, we believe it is possible to offer children access to cultural content in ways that are tailored for them. For example, we have recently expanded our Managed Accounts offering to more markets in Europe and globally.

section_path: 2 Subscription Contract Management and Consumer Autonomy
3. Protecting Without Excluding: A Holistic Approach to Minors’ Access to Culture

evidence_quotes.quote: Managed Accounts are a part of the Spotify Premium Family Plan, and allow parents to give young listeners their own space to explore music.
This includes the ability to control access to Explicit Content — which is defaulted off —
to restrict the playback of specific artists and songs.
Interactivity features are restricted on Managed Accounts, with no access to age-gated features.
no access to age-gated features.

paragraphs.text: Managed Accounts are a part of the Spotify Premium Family Plan, and allow parents to give young listeners their own space to explore music. The experience is music-only and can be controlled by Family Plan managers via their settings. This includes the ability to control access to Explicit Content — which is defaulted off — and to restrict the playback of specific artists and songs. Interactivity features are also restricted on Managed Accounts, with no access to age-gated features.

section_path: 2 Subscription Contract Management and Consumer Autonomy
3. Protecting Without Excluding: A Holistic Approach to Minors’ Access to Culture

paragraphs.text: It is vital that any new policy measures do not inadvertently deprive young people of the educational and cultural benefits that creative content can provide when offered responsibly.

section_path: 2 Subscription Contract Management and Consumer Autonomy
3. Protecting Without Excluding: A Holistic Approach to Minors’ Access to Culture

paragraphs.text: Understanding the age of users can be a useful tool to enable age-appropriate experiences online.

section_path: 2 Subscription Contract Management and Consumer Autonomy
3. Protecting Without Excluding: A Holistic Approach to Minors’ Access to Culture

evidence_quotes.quote: This technology helps us identify if users are of eligible age to access some Spotify features
age-estimation technology

paragraphs.text: Spotify offers personalised listening to music, podcasts and audiobooks for users around the world. But we know not all content or features are suitable for all audiences. To help enable age-appropriate experiences, Spotify recently expanded testing of improved age-assurance technologies to selected markets, utilising our own internal age-estimation technology and partnering with Yoti, a trusted digital identity company. This technology helps us identify if users are of eligible age to access some Spotify features, such as Music Videos that have been labelled as 18+ by rightsholders.

section_path: 2 Subscription Contract Management and Consumer Autonomy
3. Protecting Without Excluding: A Holistic Approach to Minors’ Access to Culture

paragraphs.text: We believe age assurance is a useful tool for services in their overall approach to child safety, but it is not the only tool. The use cases for age assurance vary widely, and the technology is still developing. Age assurance should be necessary and proportionate, and service providers must retain flexibility to implement it in ways that make sense.

section_path: 2 Subscription Contract Management and Consumer Autonomy
3. Protecting Without Excluding: A Holistic Approach to Minors’ Access to Culture

paragraphs.text: Legislation should reflect the diversity of online services in the digital ecosystem

section_path: 2 Subscription Contract Management and Consumer Autonomy
3. Protecting Without Excluding: A Holistic Approach to Minors’ Access to Culture

paragraphs.text: At Spotify, we believe legislation should recognise the diversity of online services, the variety of business models, and the different levels of risk that digital services present to children. Effective legislation adopts a risk-based approach, and avoids blanket requirements that risk being overly restrictive in some cases and ineffective in others. Businesses should be able to tailor their measures to protect young users — such as default settings, parental controls, and privacy safeguards — in ways that are evidence-based and context-specific.

section_path: 2 Subscription Contract Management and Consumer Autonomy
3. Protecting Without Excluding: A Holistic Approach to Minors’ Access to Culture

paragraphs.text: EU legislation addressing consumer protection in the digital sphere has expanded significantly in recent years. While we appreciate the need for rules to keep pace with technological and market developments, frequent updates create both legal uncertainty and high compliance costs – without adding demonstrable benefits to consumers.

section_path: Other Horizontal Issues

paragraphs.text: In view of the extensive body of legislation already in place, we do not believe that improving consumer protection requires a new law. Non-regulatory actions could make a real, positive difference — for example the Commission could explore a non-binding, single, living guidance hub that — whilst remaining within the boundaries of existing legislation — sets out examples of how overlapping consumer protection provisions apply in digital contexts, co-developed with industry and consumer groups.

section_path: Other Horizontal Issues

paragraphs.text: A regular evaluation (e.g. every 5 years) of rules that may be outdated, duplicative, or contradictory to other legislation could also be helpful. Such an approach could provide clarity to consumers and industry alike, helping both stakeholder communities to understand and adapt to evolving legislative frameworks and reducing the likelihood of litigation and the disruption and costs that brings to all. This type of analysis would also be important to undertake before any future regulatory reforms are considered.

section_path: Other Horizontal Issues

paragraphs.text: The Commission has proposed a number of revisions to horizontal consumer law provisions, which we would like to address:

section_path: Other Horizontal Issues

evidence_quotes.quote: the burden of proof should be reversed in cases where consumers/interested parties or authorities have disproportionate difficulty in obtaining information to prove a trader’s wrongdoing

paragraphs.text: (a) “With a view to strengthening the enforcement of consumer protection law, the burden of proof should be reversed in cases where consumers/interested parties or authorities have disproportionate difficulty in obtaining information to prove a trader’s wrongdoing” (Section 9: Horizontal Issues, Q.2)

section_path: Other Horizontal Issues

paragraphs.text: We caution strongly against the idea of reversing the burden of proof. This would fundamentally alter a core legal principle — shifting responsibility from the accuser to the accused — and would unfairly disadvantage traders. Such a core legal construct is also embedded in national laws of member states. To reverse it would lead to considerable upheaval, cutting across existing jurisprudence and societal perspectives on how the rule of law operates.

section_path: Other Horizontal Issues

paragraphs.text: There is also no clear evidence that difficulties in proving trader wrongdoing are widespread or systemic. It is unclear how the new legal processes and structures that would be required to give this effect would be created — and, crucially, how it would be

section_path: Other Horizontal Issues

paragraphs.text: enforced consistently across the EU. If enforcement bodies face challenges in obtaining information, the solution lies in better enforcement of existing laws and adequate resourcing of regulators, rather than rewriting foundational legal constructs.

section_path: Other Horizontal Issues

evidence_quotes.quote: understanding how their personal data is used

paragraphs.text: (b) “The current definition of a consumer as someone who is reasonably well-informed, observant and circumspect should be amended to better reflect the reality of consumer behaviour in the digital environment (e.g. most people not reading Terms & Conditions or understanding how their personal data is used)” (Section 9: Horizontal Issues, Q.2)

section_path: Other Horizontal Issues

evidence_quotes.quote: do not assume consumers read every detail.
There is no clear evidence that the current approach fails in digital contexts, or that “most consumers” do not read T&Cs.

paragraphs.text: This proposal appears unnecessary and unsupported by evidence. This would also create considerable upheaval, as the legal meaning of the average consumer is well-established. Courts already interpret this standard narrowly and realistically, and do not assume consumers read every detail. There is no clear evidence that the current approach fails in digital contexts, or that “most consumers” do not read T&Cs.

section_path: Other Horizontal Issues

evidence_quotes.quote: design guidance for digital checkout interfaces
“clear and conspicuous disclosures”

paragraphs.text: Under the Consumer Rights Directive, traders must present key terms clearly and proximate to the final purchase action. If traders are not complying with these obligations, that points to weak enforcement, not a legislative gap. If there is substantial evidence of a lack of understanding or awareness of key terms, the more practical and proportionate tools to address this would be via design guidance for digital checkout interfaces — as has been the case in other jurisdictions globally (for example the requirements for “clear and conspicuous disclosures” which are widely understood and fairly uniformly applied by businesses).

section_path: Other Horizontal Issues

paragraphs.text: Spotify remains fully committed to working constructively with the Commission to ensure a vibrant and fair digital ecosystem. We advocate for smart, effective regulation that strengthens consumer trust and promotes fairness. We believe the EU's priority should be simplifying the currently complex regulatory system facing European businesses, consumers and enforcement authorities, and avoiding unnecessary burdens that would hinder innovation and disadvantage the broader creative economy.

section_path: CONCLUSION

paragraphs.text: The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.
